items.passage: Many animals rely on camouflage to avoid being seen by predators or prey. The patterns on their bodies often match the colors and textures of their habitat very closely. Some species, such as cuttlefish, can change their appearance within seconds by shifting pigments in their skin. Others depend on still poses and slow movements to keep their cover from being broken. Effective camouflage gives an animal a quiet advantage that shapes survival across many generations.

Biodiversity describes the variety of living organisms within a particular region or ecosystem. Tropical rainforests are widely recognized as some of the most diverse environments on land, supporting millions of plant and animal species. The constant warmth and steady rainfall there allow many forms of life to share narrow ecological roles. When even a single species disappears, the whole network of relationships can be disturbed in unexpected ways. Protecting biodiversity helps maintain the natural systems that humans also depend on every day.

The human body has a built-in stress response that activates when a person feels threatened or pressured. The brain signals the adrenal glands to release hormones such as adrenaline and cortisol into the bloodstream. These chemicals quickly raise the heart rate, sharpen attention, and prepare the muscles for sudden action. The response was advantageous for early humans facing physical danger in their environments. Today, however, the same system can be triggered by long meetings or difficult conversations, which causes problems when stress becomes chronic.

Long before satellite systems, sailors developed careful methods for finding their way across open water. Early navigators observed the positions of the sun by day and certain bright stars by night. They also paid close attention to wave patterns, cloud shapes, and the behavior of seabirds near unfamiliar coasts. Some Pacific Island navigators memorized the routes between distant islands using oral traditions passed down for generations. Their knowledge allowed long voyages to succeed without any of the instruments modern ships now use as standard.

Earthquakes happen when rocks deep underground suddenly break and release stored energy. The pressure that builds up along the boundaries between tectonic plates can be released in just a few violent seconds. Waves of energy then travel outward through the ground and shake the surface above the source. The strength of the shaking depends on the depth of the rupture and the local soil conditions. Scientists use sensitive instruments called seismographs to record these waves and to estimate where future events may occur.

Mars has been a major target of robotic exploration for several decades now. Spacecraft sent from Earth have orbited the planet and mapped its surface in remarkable detail. Several rovers have landed safely on Mars and traveled across rocky plains while collecting samples and images. The robotic missions have found clear evidence that liquid water once flowed across the ancient surface of the planet. Future plans include returning samples to Earth and eventually sending human explorers to study the surface directly.
